Question: The probability that a male develops some form of cancer in his lifetime is .4567. The probability that a male has at least one false-positive test result, meaning the test comes back for cancer when the man does not have it, is .51. Some of the following questions do not have enough information for you to answer them. Write not enough information for those answers. Let C = a man develops cancer in his lifetime and P = a man has at least one false-positive. a) P ( C ) = ______ b) P ( P | C ) = ______ c) P ( P | C' ) = ______ d) If a test comes up positive, based upon numerical values, can you assume that man has cancer? Justify numerically and explain why or why not.
Answer: a) P ( C ) = .4567 b) not enough information c) not enough information d) no, because over half (0.51) of men have at least one false-positive text
o3 Answer:
a) P(C) = 0.4567  

b) P(P | C) = 0  
 (“P” means a false-positive; by definition a false-positive can occur only when the man does not have cancer, so the conditional probability that a man who really does have cancer experiences a false-positive is 0.)

c) P(P | C′) = 0.51  
 (The statement “The probability that a male has at least one false-positive … is .51” is exactly the probability of a false-positive given that he is cancer-free.)

d) If a test is positive, can you assume the man has cancer?  
 No.  In fact, even under the most optimistic assumption that the test always comes up positive when cancer is truly present (100 % sensitivity), the chance the man actually has cancer after a positive result is at most

  P(C | positive) = P(C) / [P(C) + P(P | C′)·P(C′)]
           = 0.4567 / [0.4567 + 0.51 · (1 – 0.4567)]
           ≈ 0.62.

 So, at best, only about 62 % of all positive results would correspond to real cancer; almost 4 out of 10 positives would still be false.  
 Because the false-positive rate is so large relative to the incidence of cancer—and because we have no information about the test’s sensitivity—you cannot conclude from a single positive test that the man definitely has cancer. Confirmatory testing is essential.

Question: Given events J and K : P ( J ) = .18; P ( K ) = .37; P ( J OR K ) = .45 a) Find P ( J AND K ). b) Find the probability of the complement of event ( J AND K ). c) Find the probability of the complement of event ( J OR K ).
Answer: a) P ( J OR K ) = P ( J ) + P ( K ) - P ( J AND K ); .45 = .18 + .37 - P ( J AND K ); solve to find P ( J AND K ) = .10 b) P (NOT ( J AND K )) = 1 - P ( J AND K ) = 1 - 010 = .90 c) P (NOT ( J OR K )) = 1 - P ( J OR K ) = 1 - .45 = .55
o3 Answer:
We use the basic rules of probability:

1. Inclusion–exclusion for two events:  
   P(J ∪ K) = P(J) + P(K) – P(J ∩ K)

2. Complement rule:  
   P(Aᶜ) = 1 – P(A)

Given: P(J) = 0.18, P(K) = 0.37, P(J ∪ K) = 0.45

a) P(J ∩ K)  
   P(J ∩ K) = P(J) + P(K) – P(J ∪ K)  
             = 0.18 + 0.37 – 0.45  
             = 0.10

b) Probability of the complement of (J ∩ K)  
   P[(J ∩ K)ᶜ] = 1 – P(J ∩ K)  
                = 1 – 0.10  
                = 0.90

c) Probability of the complement of (J ∪ K)  
   P[(J ∪ K)ᶜ] = 1 – P(J ∪ K)  
                = 1 – 0.45  
                = 0.55

2.  **The Physical Situation:** The most intuitive reason is the condition of the problem itself. The drawing is done **"with replacement."** This means that after the first card is drawn, it is returned to the deck, and the deck is shuffled. The conditions for the second draw (8 cards total, 5 of which are green) are identical to the conditions for the first draw.
